output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.4": 0.4, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.2}}

Summary: I flag a serious internal contradiction about what the paper's central contribution is, then give targeted recommendations and a suggested rewrite of the section.

## The core problem: the section claims two incompatible "central contributions"

Paragraph 1 opens: *"The central contribution of this work is a multi-agent verification protocol... that improves end-to-end execution accuracy..."* — framed as a systems/accuracy contribution, with the 8.2-point gain as the headline result.

Paragraph 3 then says: *"The central claim of this paper is that current LLM-based NL2SQL systems are systematically miscalibrated... The 8.2-point accuracy gain is then a corollary of the calibration analysis."* — framed as a measurement/diagnostic contribution, with accuracy demoted to a corollary.

These cannot both be the "central" contribution. A reader reaching paragraph 3 will feel bait-and-switched, and a reviewer will (rightly) ask which paper they are being asked to evaluate. You must pick one and rewrite paragraph 1 to match. My recommendation: commit to the calibration framing, because (a) it is the more novel claim, (b) the protocol-as-accuracy-tool framing competes directly with a crowded field of NL2SQL agent papers, and (c) you have already done the analytical work (SCCE, AUROC, Spearman) that supports the calibration story.

## Specific issues

**1. The "corollary" claim is not actually argued.** Paragraph 3 asserts the 8.2-point gain "is then a corollary of the calibration analysis," but nothing in the section shows that the accuracy gain follows from the calibration finding. As written, the gain comes from the verification protocol; the calibration analysis is a separate observation made during the audit. Either (a) demonstrate the mechanism — e.g., "verification recovers accuracy *because* it re-ranks candidates that the base model was overconfident about, and we show X% of recovered queries come from the high-confidence-but-wrong tail" — or (b) drop the word "corollary" and say the two findings are complementary.

**2. "Multi-agent verification... surfaces calibration failures that would otherwise be invisible" is under-supported.** The section reports AUROC < 0.55 and Spearman ρ = −0.21 for per-critic confidence on cross-schema queries. But those numbers describe how bad existing confidences are — not that the multi-agent setup is what surfaced them. In principle, you could have measured AUROC against any baseline's self-reported confidence without the debate machinery. State explicitly what the protocol contributes to the calibration finding that a simpler probe could not.

**3. SCCE is introduced but its motivation and properties are compressed.** "Adjusts ECE for the heterogeneous prior over per-database difficulty" is dense. Two things readers will want: (i) why standard ECE fails on cross-schema benchmarks specifically (presumably because per-schema sample sizes vary and difficulty is non-uniform), and (ii) what "admits a per-schema decomposition without requiring a held-out per-schema validation pool" buys them operationally. One sentence each would help.

**4. Evidence specificity.** Numbers are reported without anchoring: 8.2 points over *which* single-shot baseline (model, decoding, prompt)? "Comparable inference cost" — comparable how (tokens, wall-clock, dollar)? "Oracle SQL selector" — selector over what candidate pool? These matter because the 8.2-point claim is load-bearing for paragraph 1 and reviewers will probe it.

**5. The Spider-DK claim is the strongest single sentence in the section and is buried.** A negative Spearman correlation between reported confidence and correctness on join-heavy queries is striking — confidence is not just uninformative, it's actively misleading. Consider promoting this to the lead of the calibration paragraph.

**6. Structural ordering.** Currently: protocol → critic roles → calibration observation → reframing. If you commit to the calibration framing, invert: open with the calibration failure as the diagnosis, introduce the protocol as the instrument that made the diagnosis tractable, then report SCCE and accuracy as two consequences. The current order forces the reader to discard their model of the paper at paragraph 3.

**7. Minor prose.**
- "shape-consistent with the foreign-key graph" — "shape-consistent" is jargon; "structurally consistent" or a brief gloss would land better.
- "essentially uninformative" — quantified two sentences later, so "essentially" is dead wood.
- "in the course of the audit we observed" → "the audit revealed" (active, shorter).
- "ultimately reframe the contribution" — "ultimately" foreshadows the bait-and-switch; if you restructure per (6), this transition disappears.

## Suggested rewrite (committing to the calibration framing)

> Current LLM-based NL2SQL pipelines are systematically miscalibrated on cross-schema queries: their self-reported confidence is uninformative at best and misleading at worst. On BIRD-bench, per-candidate confidence achieves AUROC below 0.55 for distinguishing executable from non-executable SQL; on the Spider-DK join-heavy slice, confidence is *anti-correlated* with correctness (Spearman ρ = −0.21). Single-shot generation cannot repair this, because the failure is in the ranking signal itself, not in the candidate pool.
>
> Our contribution is a multi-agent verification protocol that exposes and partially corrects this miscalibration. Three critics — a schema-grounding critic that checks referenced columns against the catalog, a join-cardinality critic that tests structural consistency against the foreign-key graph, and an execution-trace critic that runs the candidate on a table sample — produce independent judgments that a fourth aggregator reconciles. Because the critics ground in artifacts the base model does not consult at generation time (catalog, FK graph, execution trace), their disagreement pattern recovers a calibration signal the base model cannot.
>
> We quantify residual miscalibration with a schema-conditional calibration error (SCCE), which adjusts ECE for heterogeneous per-database difficulty and decomposes per-schema without a held-out per-schema validation pool — a practical requirement on benchmarks like BIRD where some schemas contribute only a handful of queries. As a downstream consequence, the protocol recovers 8.2 percentage points of execution accuracy over a single-shot baseline of comparable token cost, closing roughly half the gap to an oracle selector over the same candidate pool.

This version (a) leads with the diagnostic finding, (b) explains *why* the multi-agent setup is the right instrument for it (the critics see things the base model doesn't), (c) gives SCCE a concrete motivation, and (d) keeps the accuracy number but in its proper role.

## Uncertainty

I have not verified the BIRD or Spider-DK numbers, nor whether SCCE is genuinely novel (item 4 in the dim list is therefore weakly covered) — a prior-work check against work on calibration in code/SQL generation and on schema-stratified evaluation metrics would be worth doing before claiming SCCE as a contribution. The mechanism claim I propose in the rewrite ("critics ground in artifacts the base model does not consult at generation time") is plausible but should be checked against your actual ablations before you commit to it in print.
